name: Cyr Wheel
description: The Cyr wheel is a human-sized metal ring (aluminum or steel, typically 10-15 cm taller than the performer) inside which the artist stands, grips the rim, and generates rotation through weight shifts and body positioning. Named after Daniel Cyr, who popularized it through Cirque Eloize in 1997, it became an official competition discipline in 2013 under the International Wheel Gymnastics federation. Judges score technical programs and free programs on execution, difficulty, and artistic impression.

thumbnail.alt: Performer spinning inside a large metal Cyr wheel on a stage

media.title: Isaac Hou - incredible Cyr wheel street performance in Taiwan

sources.title: 250 years of circus: the truth behind the Cyr Wheel

time_to_competence.notes: Basic waltz (three-beat rotation) and coin spin take weeks of practice. Staying inside the wheel without falling out is the first challenge.

time_to_expert.notes: Performance-quality acts with aerial moves, spirals, and seamless transitions require 5-10 years. Cirque du Soleil acts represent peak mastery.

feedback_delay.notes: Loss of control is immediately apparent through wobble, deceleration, or falling out of the wheel.

failure_cost.notes: Falls from a spinning wheel cause bruises, friction burns, and joint impacts. Serious injuries are uncommon with proper progression and mats.

cognitive_demand.notes: Requires understanding of angular momentum, weight shift timing, and spatial awareness while disoriented by rotation.

physical_demand.notes: Full-body engagement: grip strength, core rotation, shoulder stability, and leg power. The 15kg wheel demands constant force to maintain movement.

prerequisite_knowledge.notes: Prior experience in dance, gymnastics, or circus arts accelerates learning. No formal academic prerequisites.

measurability.notes: Competition scores combine execution, difficulty, and artistry. Technical elements are codified but artistic impression remains subjective.

perishability.notes: The vestibular adaptation to constant spinning degrades with disuse. Grip strength and movement patterns fade without regular practice.

precision_required.notes: Hand and foot placement on the rim must be exact. Weight shifts of centimeters determine whether a spiral continues or collapses.

ambiguity.notes: Competition has codified difficulty values for elements, but artistic expression introduces subjectivity.

instruction_dependency.notes: Self-teaching is dangerous and inefficient. Circus schools provide essential spotting, progression methodology, and technique correction.

environmental_variability.notes: Floor surface dramatically affects grip and rolling behavior. Outdoor performance adds wind and uneven terrain. Temperature affects metal grip.

concurrent_skills.notes: Simultaneously managing rotation speed, spatial orientation, grip transitions, body positioning, and artistic expression while disoriented.

plateau_density.notes: Significant plateaus at each new rotation type. Transitioning from waltz to coin, from coin to spiral, each requires fundamental relearning.

domain_evolution.notes: Rapidly evolving since Daniel Cyr's 1997 introduction. Competition rules formalized in 2013. New elements are still being invented.

observability.notes: A performer spinning gracefully inside a giant ring is visually arresting. The apparatus itself commands attention before any movement begins.

luck_variance.notes: Controlled environment with predictable physics. Floor surface is the main variable. Equipment is consistent.

social_coordination.notes: Almost exclusively solo. Duo Cyr wheel acts exist but are rare. Training may involve spotters.

methodology_notes: Competition history from International Wheel Gymnastics federation records. Training progression from major circus schools (ENC Montreal, ESAC Belgium). Apparatus specifications from Wikipedia and circus equipment manufacturers.
